quote: There's lots of ways to be, as a person. And some people express their deep appreciation in different ways. But one of the ways that I believe people express their appreciation to the rest of humanity is to make something wonderful and put it out there.
notes: From Jobs's remarks at an internal Apple meeting on October 23, 2007. Verified via the Steve Jobs Archive (stevejobsarchive.com/stories/on-the-origin-of-make-something-wonderful). Featured in Quote Unquote #1, "Making something wonderful."

quote: If ever there is tomorrow when we're not together… there is something you must always remember. You are braver than you believe, stronger than you seem, and smarter than you think. But the most important thing is, even if we're apart… I'll always be with you.
attribution: Christopher Robin, Pooh's Grand Adventure: The Search for Christopher Robin (1997)
notes: Written by Carter Crocker and Karl Geurs for the Disney film. Popularly misattributed to A.A. Milne (dead 41 years before the line existed) and usually shortened to "You're braver than you believe..." with the roles flipped so Pooh says it to Christopher Robin. Featured in Quote Unquote #2.

From Ulrich's scholarly article "Vertuous Women Found: New England Ministerial Literature, 1668–1735" (American Quarterly, 1976) — an observation that dutiful women are omitted from the historical record, not a call to misbehave. Frequently misattributed to Marilyn Monroe or Eleanor Roosevelt, with its meaning inverted. Featured in Quote Unquote #3.

quote: I can accept failure, everyone fails at something. But I can't accept not trying.
notes: The title creed of Michael Jordan with Mark Vancil, I Can't Accept Not Trying: Michael Jordan on the Pursuit of Excellence (HarperSanFrancisco, 1994). Genuinely his, but usually shared without its source or ghostwriter. Featured in Quote Unquote #4.

quote: He stepped down, trying not to look long at her, as if she were the sun, yet he saw her, like the sun, even without looking.
notes: The scene is Levin watching Kitty skate — not Vronsky and Anna at the train platform, as it is often miscaptioned. Wording is the Garnett/Maude-era translation; Pevear and Volokhonsky render it differently. Featured in Quote Unquote #5.

attribution: Darth Vader, The Empire Strikes Back (1980)
notes: The actual line. Popularly misremembered as "Luke, I am your father" — a Mandela Effect; a 2023 YouGov poll found 63% recalled the wrong version and only 17% the right one. Featured in Quote Unquote #6.

quote: I disapprove of what you say, but I will defend to the death your right to say it.
notes: Written by Hall (pen name S. G. Tallentyre) in The Friends of Voltaire (1906) to characterize Voltaire's attitude, not to quote him; she later confirmed the words were "my own expression." Universally misattributed to Voltaire. Featured in Quote Unquote #7.

quote: The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over again and expecting different results.
notes: Not found in Einstein's works; listed as a misattribution in The Ultimate Quotable Einstein (Princeton University Press, 2010). Earliest documented traces are a 1981 Narcotics Anonymous pamphlet and Rita Mae Brown's novel Sudden Death (1983). Featured in Quote Unquote #8.

quote: The earth does not belong to us; we belong to the earth… Whatever befalls the earth befalls the sons and daughters of the earth. We did not weave the web of life; we are merely a strand in it.
attribution: Attributed to Chief Seattle (actually written by Ted Perry)
notes: The widely circulated environmental "speech" was written by screenwriter Ted Perry for the film Home (1972), loosely inspired by an unreliable 1887 reconstruction of a speech Chief Seattle may have given around 1854. The environmental imagery is Perry's, not Seattle's; the misattribution was exposed by The New York Times in 1992. Featured in Quote Unquote #9.

quote: The prize is not the trophy, it is who you become as you earned it.
notes: A paraphrase — a modern condensation of Jim Rohn's teaching, which Rohn himself credited to his mentor Earl Shoaff. Documented in Jim Rohn, 7 Strategies for Wealth and Happiness (Prima Publishing, 1985). Featured in Quote Unquote #10, "The trophy isn't the prize."

quote: Okay, Houston, we've had a problem here.
notes: The actual air-to-ground transmission (past tense); commander Jim Lovell then repeated it. The famous present-tense "Houston, we have a problem" comes from the 1995 film Apollo 13, which changed the tense for dramatic effect. Featured in Quote Unquote #11.

quote: Be the change you wish to see in the world.
notes: Not found in Gandhi's collected works; traced to educator Arleen Lorrance's "Love Project" (c. 1970). Gandhi's related idea appears in Indian Opinion (1913): "We but mirror the world... If we could change ourselves, the tendencies in the world would also change." Featured in Quote Unquote #12.

quote: Happy families are all alike; every unhappy family is unhappy in its own way.
notes: The novel's opening line (1878), quoted in the Constance Garnett (1901) translation. The "Anna Karenina Principle" was coined by Jared Diamond in Guns, Germs, and Steel (1997). Featured in Quote Unquote #13.

quote: The mark of a man was to be able to say at the end, 'I've done my share.'
notes: Quoted by David P. Morgan in Trains Magazine, July 1983, p. 4. Featured in Quote Unquote #14.

quote: All your base are belong to us.
notes: From a non-professional in-house English translation of Toaplan's Zero Wing (arcade 1989; the intro cutscene appeared in the 1991 European Mega Drive port). Spoken by the antagonist CATS, it became one of the first viral internet memes (2000–2001). Featured in Quote Unquote #15.
